coredata.dc:title: “Adsorption of gases on doped graphene quantum dots with (TM = Ni, Pd, and Pt): DFT and TD-DFT investigation”
coredata.dc:creator: Taha, H.O.
El Mahdy, A.M.
Lebda, H.I.
coredata.dc:description: The effects of TM (Ni, Pd, and Pt) doping and the adsorption of NO, NO2, and NH3 gases on the electrical as well as optical properties of the armchair-hexagonal (AHEX) graphene quantum dots have been investigated using DFT and TD-DFT calculations at the B3lyp/SDD level of the theory. TM-doped AHEX GQD enhances the adsorption energies for NO, NO2, and NH3 gases. Adsorption of NO, NO2, and NH3 gases changes the Eg of Ni-AHEX (−36.55 %, −45.14 %, 13.49 %), Pd-AHEX (−42.76 %, −87.04 %, 8.48 %), and Pt-AHEX (−37.05 %, −35.91 %, 15.83 %), respectively. In addition, ΔG was negative for all configurations indicating that gas molecule adsorption on TM-doped AHEX GQD is always thermodynamically advantageous. According to the UV–Vis examination, the adsorption of NO, NO2, and NH3 gases changes the material from UV-active to visible-active. In conclusion, TM-doped AHEX GQD is a viable candidate material for application as a sensor of harmful NO, NO2, and NH3 gases.

head.ce:title: “Adsorption of gases on doped graphene quantum dots with (TM = Ni, Pd, and Pt): DFT and TD-DFT investigation”

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Education, Ain Shams University, Roxy, 11575, Cairo, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Faculty of Education, Ain Shams University, Roxy, 11575, Cairo, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effects of TM (Ni, Pd, and Pt) doping and the adsorption of NO, NO2, and NH3 gases on the electrical as well as optical properties of the armchair-hexagonal (AHEX) graphene quantum dots have been investigated using DFT and TD-DFT calculations at the B3lyp/SDD level of the theory. TM-doped AHEX GQD enhances the adsorption energies for NO, NO2, and NH3 gases. Adsorption of NO, NO2, and NH3 gases changes the Eg of Ni-AHEX (−36.55 %, −45.14 %, 13.49 %), Pd-AHEX (−42.76 %, −87.04 %, 8.48 %), and Pt-AHEX (−37.05 %, −35.91 %, 15.83 %), respectively. In addition, ΔG was negative for all configurations indicating that gas molecule adsorption on TM-doped AHEX GQD is always thermodynamically advantageous. According to the UV–Vis examination, the adsorption of NO, NO2, and NH3 gases changes the material from UV-active to visible-active. In conclusion, TM-doped AHEX GQD is a viable candidate material for application as a sensor of harmful NO, NO2, and NH3 gases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Numerous additional carbon nanoforms, including hybrid nanostructures, have been discovered as a result of research into fullerenes and their fascinating features since their discovery in 1985. These include carbon tubes, carbon onions, graphene, graphdiyne, and carbon nanotori [1–6]. Consequently, there are various sensitive programs of carbon nanostructures, like fuel line sensors, pollutant molecule adsorption, electric-powered batteries, hydrogen adsorption, digital electronics, etc. It is critical to trap gases such as NO, NO2, NH3, and many more. In our environment, a lot of industrial operations produce greenhouse gases including CO2, NO2, and CH4. Thus, the need for carbon nanostructures for quick green operations has grown as a result of ecological issues and climate change facing humanity [7]. Carbon atoms are arranged in a dense two-dimensional lattice structure called graphene. This material has received a lot of attention because of its unusual nanostructure and intrinsic characteristics, such as high thermal conductivity and inexpensive manufacturing costs [8], and good electrical, thermal, mechanical, electronic, and optical properties [9–11]. Its large specific surface area, good pliability, high chemical stability, high porosity, and outstanding electron-transport qualities are among its other noteworthy characteristics [12,13]. One of the distinguishing characteristics of Graphene is that it is a promising material for use in a variety of nanotechnological fields, including batteries [14], supercapacitors [15], field-effect transistors [16], fuel cells [17], and electrochemical sensors [18]. Moreover, organic light-emitting devices and solar cells [19] use thin, transparent graphene sheets as electrodes [20]. Additionally, due to its excellent biocompatibility and strong electro-catalytic activity, this material has led to interesting findings in bioscience [21,22]. However, much research has shown that intrinsic graphene poorly bodily adsorbs maximum gas molecules [23,24], so studies of doped graphene [25] and vacancy complaint graphene [26] proposed to solve this problem. Numerous studies have demonstrated that doping atoms or defects into graphene may effectively improve the price switch between gas molecules and graphene, enhancing certain gas molecules capacity to adsorb graphene, and boost the sensitivity and selectivity of graphene sensors [27,28]. Pristine graphene has a limited sensitivity to typical gas molecules including CO, CO2, CH4, N2, NO2, NH3, H2, and H2O [29–31], thus limiting its uses for the detection of specific gas molecules [32]. They suggested that the electrical and magnetic properties of different nanomaterials may be tuned through the use of dopants and defects [33–41]. Humans can be poisoned by NO, NO2, and NH3, NO is less toxic than NO2, but it is unstable and readily interacts with oxygen to generate NO2. It can induce acute lung damage, pneumonitis, and fulminant pulmonary edema even at low concentrations [42]. In contrast to these two gases, NO2 does not pose a direct hazard to human health. However, it is classified as an air pollutant since it is one of the greenhouse gases, with an absorption of infrared radiation 270 times greater than that of carbon dioxide (CO2) [43]. Furthermore, it indirectly impacts the ozone (O3) layer during photochemical reactions. There are a few more nitrogen oxides in the environment, and they all play a significant part in the photochemistry of the atmosphere. NH3 gas is released daily into the atmosphere and rain spouts due to its many applications, including creating plastics, preparing nitric acid, cooling gas, processing food, fabricating fiber, agricultural fertilizers, and pharmaceutical preparation [44,45]. NH3 is a toxic, colorless, and hazardous gas with a distinct odor [46,47] that causes nose and throat discomfort, eye irritation, respiratory system injury, skin damage, and death [44,47]. As a result, it is vital to look for new devices capable of disposing of such gases as well as detecting them. Previous density functional theory (DFT) studies showed considerable differences in the interaction of different metal atoms with graphene [48] and MoS2 [49]. Kunjie Li et al. [50] examined the adsorption of CO and NO gas molecules on Pd- and Pt-decorated single-walled carbon nanotubes using first-principles simulations. They found that there is a considerable binding energy and charge transfer when CO and NO gas molecules are chemisorbed at the Pd and Pt surfaces. N. Farid and S. Majid [51] investigated the adsorptions of O2 with Fe-, Co-, Ni-, Ru-, Rh-, Pd-, OS-, Ir-, and Pt-doped graphene using density functional theory calculations. Those showed that the oxygen molecule (O2) is chemisorbed at the doped graphene sheets with large adsorption energies, which changes the potential of using these materials as a toxic-gas sensor. DFT based on first principles was used by S. Mehdi Aghaei et al. [52] to study the adsorption attitudes of dangerous molecules of gases (NO, CO, NO2, and NH3) at the graphene-like boron carbide (BC3). M. S. M. Shukri et al. [53] used DFT simulations inside the generalized gradient approximation to examine the sensitivity of Pd-doped graphene from tiny gas molecules CO to NO. They hypothesized that doping Pd on the vacant graphene surface may enhance the adsorption energy of CO and NO gas molecules. Saidi [54] study the evolution of the morphology and atomic structure of (Pt)n clusters of the MoS2 [001] face for n
≤
12 using density functional theory calculations. They found that the transition of platinum nanoparticle growth from the 2D to 3D state occurs at n = 5. X. Gao et al. [55] examined, using basic principles and the DFT approach, the adsorptions of graphene to H2S and CH4 molecules both before and after modification. They found that vacancy defect graphene (DG) exhibits excellent adsorption capability for H2S and that a single vacancy defect increases the strength of contact between graphene and adsorbed molecules. Demir and M.F. Fellah [56] studied the adsorption and sensing of nitrogen monoxide (NO) on Pt, Ni, and Pd-doped (6, 0) SWCNTs using density functional theory calculations at WB97XD using the basis sets of 6–31 G (d, p) and LanL2DZ. They stated that the Pt-doped carbon nanotubes could aid with the design and fabrication of NO sensors at room temperature. Kishnani et al. [57] investigated the adsorption and sensing of H2 molecules at the surface of Pd-doped and Pd-decorated graphene (G) using density functional theory (DFT) computations. They proposed that Pd-decorated graphene is better for H2 gas detection, and that Pd-doped graphene has a lower adsorption energy of −4.3 eV than Pd-decorated graphene, which is −0.44 eV. W. Osman et al. [58] used density functional theory to investigate the electrical and magnetic characteristics of graphene quantum dots doped with alkali metals (Li, Na, and K). They suggested that doped graphene flakes are attractive candidates for spintronic devices due to the improved spin polarization achieved. R. F. d. Menezes et al. [59] studied the electrical structure and molecular dynamics of CO, NO2, SO2, and NH3 adsorption on graphene Quantum Dots doped with boron (B), nitrogen (N), and aluminum (Al). They suggested that quantum dots doped with boron (B), nitrogen (N), and aluminum (Al) are considerably suitable for application in NO2 gas sensors.

In this study, DFT calculations at the B3LYP/SDD level are used to examine the adsorption of NO, NO2, and NH3 on pure armchair-hexagonal (AHEX) graphene quantum dots as well as armchair-hexagonal (AHEX) graphene quantum dots doped with transition metals (Ni, Pd, and Pt). The theoretical model is presented in the next part in order to accomplish this objective. Section 3.1 investigates the influence of TM doping on the characteristics of AHEX graphene quantum dots. The explanation of the NO, NO2, and NH3 adsorption on pure and doped AHEX graphene quantum dots follows in Section 3.2. To interpret our findings, we make use of adsorption energies, frontier orbitals, and charge transfer through NBO analyses, dipole moment, partial density of states (PDOS), thermodynamic parameters, polarizability, hyperpolarizability, and molecular electrostatic potentials (MEPs). Section 3.3 computed the UV–Vis absorption spectra for the isolated AHEX systems and their systems using time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) calculations to investigate the influence of doping and adsorbed gases on the absorbance of the studied clusters. Section 4 then delivers the conclusion.

In this study, the armchair hexagonal (AHEX) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were chosen as the model flakes, as shown in Fig. 1 . We constructed a GQD consisting of 42 carbon and 18 hydrogen atoms. As shown in Fig. 1, transition metal atoms (Ni, Pd, and Pt) are bonded to the top position (T) of the flake, or a carbon atom inside the flake on the (U) upper or (L) vertex position of the hexagon. The abbreviation TM-AHEX stands for TM transition metal (TM = Ni, Pd, and Pt) attached to the E position. In contrast, AHEX-P-TM represents doped AHEX with a TM transition metal atom in position P, where P]U or L. All computations at the DFT level of theory were performed using the Gaussian (G09) suite of algorithms [60]. Due to the presence of heavy transition elements in the structures, only a few basis sets produced a satisfactory result. The theoretical computations in this study are performed using the density functional theorem (DFT) at Becke-3-functionals with Lee-Yang-Parr (B3LYP) and SDD (Stuttgart Dresden triple zeta ECPs [effective-core potential]) basis sets [61–64]. The SDD basis set was used in our calculations because of the presence of heavy transition metals in our systems. The success and failure of B3LYP have resulted in the development of numerous new hybrid functionals. Several meta-functionals were developed in Minnesota (2005), and they are our functionals with high precision. The Minnesota functional family contains local (M06-L) and hybrid meta-GGAs [65,66], and provides the best dependability with wide chemical applications. In main-group and transition metal chemistry, the local density functional, M06-L [65], is well-performing and parametrized to fulfil the uniform electron-gas limit. The M06–2X [66] outperforms the M06-L and M06 in terms of main-group thermochemistry, barrier heights, and non-covalent interactions.

Using the Gaussian (G09) software suite, all calculations at the DFT level of theory were carried out [60]. Adsorbed gases, sorbent clusters, and gas-cluster complexes had their densities of states (DOS) and projected densities of states (PDOS) calculated using the Gauss Sum software [81]. NBO version 3.1 [82] evaluated atomic charges. The optimized geometry was seen using the linked Gauss-View (GV5) [83] application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of NO, NO2, NH3 and TM (TM = Ni, Pd and Pt) on AHEX GQD
ce:section.ce:para: The electronic configuration, spin state, spin multiplicity, electronic state, and total electronic energy of TM in the gas phase are displayed in Table S1. Next, we determined the doping location of a TM atom on AHEX-GQD. Metal atoms can be attached to surfaces in three distinct locations: top (T), bottom (L), and upper (U). Fig. 2 depicts the optimal structures of AHEX doped with metals (Ni, Pd, and Pt). AHEX-Ni, AHEX-Pd, and AHEX-Pt optimal configurations with metal atoms bound to the T location, respectively, are shown in (Fig. 2e–g). (Fig. 2h-m) displays the doped AHEX's optimal structure at the L and U locations.

As illustrated in (Fig. 2e–g), attaching transition metals (TM) to the edge of the GQDs has no influence on the geometric structure. The C–C bond lengths (dC-C) for AHEX linked to Ni, Pd, and Pt in Table 2 range from 1.393
Å
to 1.468
Å
. This is similar to the C–C bond length (1.421
Å
) in bulk graphene. For a deeper look at these findings, when transition metals (TM) replace one carbon atom in a flake, they have a significant impact on the structure and behave as dopants. Large differences in dC-C and dC-M from the initial dC-C in AHEX are seen; for instance, in AHEX-L-Pd, dC-C ranges are from 1.38 to 1.49 Å and dC-M from 1.77 1.98 to 2.05 2.02 Å. These changes replace part of the C–C bonds and suggest a significant structural change in AHEX.

In terms of binding energy definition equation (1), negative binding energy indicates the stability of the resulting structures; thus, we infer that all of the systems under consideration are stable based on the negative values of EB (see Table 2). As an example, for a full study, we chose the most exothermic adsorption configuration in which transition metals (TM = Ni, Pd, and Pt) are attached to the T position of the GQDs. As shown in Table 2, the estimated binding energies per atom for AHEX (C42H18) are Eb = −6.901 eV, which compares favorably to −7.05 eV [86], whereas doping reduces to −6.897, −6.854, and −6.872 eV for Ni-AHEX, Pd-AHEX, and Pt-AHEX, respectively. The computed ionization potential (IP) and electron affinity (EA) for AHEX GQD are 5.484 and 1.822 eV, respectively, as indicated in Table 3 . Furthermore, IP decreased by 0.194, 0.098, and 0.018 eV for Ni-AHEX, Pd-AHEX, and Pt-AHEX, respectively, while EA increased by 0.349, 0.275, and 0.581 eV. Furthermore, η, S, and ω for the pure AHEX GQD are 1.831 eV, 0.273 eV-1, and 3.644 eV, respectively. Additionally, η values for Ni-AHEX, Pd-AHEX, and Pt-AHEX were decreased to 1.560, 1.644, and 1.644 eV, S values were increased to 0.321, 0.304, and 0.326 eV-1, and ω values increased to 4.462, 4.257, and 5.055 eV, respectively. According to the description above, all of the examined structures are stable; however, doping increases the stability of the Ni-AHEX, Pd-AHEX, and Pt-AHEX structures, increasing their reactivity relative to the AHEX GQD structure.

The atomic charges for the examined structure were calculated using Natural Bond Orbital (NBO) analysis. The charge of the TM atom in Ni-AHEX, Pd-AHEX, and Pt-AHEX structures is 0.497, 0.303, and 0.206 |e|, respectively, whereas the charge of the closest C atom is −0.359, −0.178, and −0.136|e|. The positive charges on transition metal atoms and the negative charges on the closest carbon atoms determined from the NBO calculation ensure charge transfer from transition metal atoms to the AHEX GQD, as shown in Table 2. This indicates that the TM atoms create the mixed sp2d hybridization by transferring electrons to the nearby C atoms on AHEX GQD, where the d orbitals of the TM atoms overlap with the sp2 orbitals of the TM C bonds. Due to the charge transfer activity, the TM atoms become cationic and form substantial heteropolar bonds with the neighboring C atoms. As a result, the HOMO-LUMO energy gap (Eg) values for Ni-AHEX, Pd-AHEX, and Pt-AHEX were reduced to 15 %, 10.2 %, and 16.4 % of those for the pure AHEX GQD structure, respectively, whereas the dipole moment values climbed to 3.004, 1.744, and 0.148 Debye, respectively. The elevated dipole moment of the doped structures suggests a higher responsiveness to the environment around it.

Fig. S1 depicts the density of states (DOS) for free AHEX GQD and doped AHEX GQD structures. Table 3 summarizes and illustrates the frontier orbitals (HOMO and LUMO). The free AHEX GQD structure has a dispersed overall surface, as seen in (Fig. S1a), and its HOMO and LUMO are −5.484 and −1.822 eV, respectively. In doped structures, the LUMO surfaces are concentrated around the TM atoms, as seen in structures (Fig. S1b, c, and d), and the HOMO surfaces are unfocalized around the TM atoms. As a result, the HOMO in the doped Ni-AHEX, Pd-AHEX, and Pt-AHEX structures is −5.290, −5.386, and −5.466 eV, and the LUMO is −2.171, −2.097, and −2.403 eV, respectively. So, for Ni-AHEX, Pd-AHEX, and Pt-AHEX, the HOMO-LUMO energy gap was decreased to 3.119 eV, 3.288 eV, and 3.063 eV, respectively, while the Fermi level was decreased to −3.731 eV, −3.741 eV, and −3.935 eV.

denotes electrical conductivity, Eg the energy gap, k the Boltzmann constant, and T the temperature. The electric conductivity of the doped Ni-AHEX, Pd-AHEX, and Pt-AHEX structures is projected to be higher than that of the AHEX GQD structure.

Aside from our previous discussion, doping has a significant impact on the electrical characteristics of the AHEX GQD system. As a result of doping, the IP values get lower and the eA values of the doped structures become higher, thus enabling the substrate clusters and the adsorbing gases to transfer charges more easily. While doping reduces chemical hardness (η) values while increasing softness (S) and dipole moments (μ), this confirms the doped systems' strong reactivity. As a result, it may improve its adsorptivity to NO, NO2, and NH3 gas molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of NO, NO2, and NH3 molecules on doped TM-AHEX GQD
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction of the doped TM-AHEX GQD (TM = Ni, Pd, and Pt) with the adsorbed gases, NO, NO2 and NH3 gas molecules will be studied in this section. Without imposing any symmetrical restrictions, the optimal structures for the gases under consideration were created. The results were then compared using the density functionals M06-L, M06–2X, and B3LYP. Be aware that the basis set superposition error (BSSE), which was calculated using the counterpoise approach using the density functionals M06-L, M06–2X, and B3LYP to correct the Eads.

The estimated adsorption energies of the NO, NO2, and NH3-TM-AHEX GQD systems are provided in (Table 4 a, b, and c) to evaluate the general stability of the three adsorbed complexes. Fig. 3 shows the most stable adsorption structures for NO, NO2, and NH3 (Table 4a, b, c). and Fig. 4 both provide summaries and illustrations of the optimal distances for three gases: TM-AHEX GQD and TM-Gases.

(Table 4a, b, c) shows that the adsorption energies for NO, NO2, and NH3-TM-AHEX GQD systems are exothermic, indicating that the NO, NO2, and NH3 have strong interactions with the TM-AHEX GQD, resulting in stable adsorbed structures. The bond length (DN-O) of the adsorbed NO is around 1.189–1.205 Å, which is somewhat longer than that of the isolated NO molecule (1.14 Å) according to (Table 4 a, b, c). This suggests that the initial N–O bond of the NO molecule is weakened during the adsorption process. Additionally, in the current NO2-TM-AHEX GQD systems, the N–O distance (DN-O) is stretched from 1.17 Å in the isolated NO2 gas molecule to around 1.889–2.057 Å. According to (Table 4a, b, c), the bond length of the adsorbed NO (DN-O) is around 1.189–1.205 Å, which is somewhat longer than the bond length of the isolated NO molecule (1.14 Å), implying that the adsorption process reduces the NO molecule's original N–O bond. The N–H distance (DN-H) is also increased from 0.98 Å in the alone NH3 gas molecule to around 1.017–1.019 Å in the current NH3 - TM- AHEX GQD systems.

(Table 4a) displays the range of adsorption energies for NO on TM-AHEX GQD, which were computed at B3LYP to be between −1.447 and −2.370 eV. It is obvious that the relative magnitude order of the adsorption energies follows the following order: NO–Pt-AHEX (−2.370 eV) > NO–Ni-AHEX (−1.706 eV) > NO–Pd-AHEX (−1.447 eV). The high adsorption energies and short bond length indicated that the molecule had a strong interaction with TM- AHEX GQD. When compared to the results in (Table 4a), the M06–2X functional with high Hartree-Fock exchange (54 % exact exchange) underestimated the adsorption energies Eads, whereas the M06-L functional with just local exchange overestimated the adsorption energies Eads. At B3LYP, M06–2X, and M06-L, Pt-AHEX had the strongest interactions with NO molecules. At M06–2X, Ni-AHEX has the lowest interaction with the NO molecule.

According to calculations performed at B3LYP, the adsorption energies for NO2 on TM- AHEX GQD fall within the range (−1.041 to −2.370 eV) in (Table 4b). The sequence of their relative magnitudes was as follows: NO2–Pt-AHEX (−2.405 eV), NO2–Ni-AHEX (−2.162 eV), and then NO2–Pd-AHEX (−1.041 eV). The NO2 molecule experienced a strong interaction with AHEX GQD, as evidenced by the significant adsorption energies and short bond lengths. The NO2 molecule and Pt-AHEX bind most strongly at B3LYP and M06–2X. M06-L. While the Pd-AHEX has the lowest interaction with the NO2 molecule at the B3LYP, M06–2X, and M06-L, respectively.

The calculated adsorption energies for NH3 on TM - AHEX GQD are shown in (Table 4c) and are in the range (−1.175 to −1.452 eV). The sequence of their comparative energies was arranged as follows: NH3–Ni-AHEX (−1.452 eV) > NH3–Pt-AHEX (−1.371 eV) > NH3–Pd-AHEX (−1.175 eV). The NH3 molecule had a significant interaction with AHEX GQD, as evidenced by the substantial adsorption energies and short bond lengths. At B3LYP, M06–2X, and M06-L, Ni-AHEX has the strongest interactions with the NH3 molecule. While at the B3LYP, M06–2X, and M06-L, the Pd-AHEX exhibits the lowest contact with the NH3 molecule. The energies of adsorption except for the M06-L level, the Eads of NO2 adsorbed systems are all lower than those of both NO and NH3 adsorbed TM- AHEX systems, demonstrating that NO2 is more stable than NO and NH3 on TM- AHEX. The distances between the N and TM atoms in NH3 are all greater than those between the N and TM atoms in NO and NO2, showing that the increasingly electronegative N atom in NH3 attracts more electrons than the N atom in NO and NO2.

Chemical bond investigation revealed that the NH3 interacts with the TM- AHEX significantly more powerfully than it does with pure AHEX, demonstrating that the TM- AHEX is suitable to adsorb NO, NO2, and NH3 molecules. All of the structural stability test findings indicate that the AHEX GQD may become functionalized in the presence of a doped TM in the AHEX GQD wall, making it a viable material for nan-device applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The adsorbates' and substrates' dipole moments
ce:section.ce:para: We examined the dipole moments for AHEX, NO, NO2, NH3, TM-AHEX, NO-TM-AHEX, NO2-TM-AHEX, and NH3-TM-AHEX. Table 1 illustrates that the dipole moment of the pure AHEX is 0.0021 Debye, whereas for the molecules of NO, NO2, and NH3, it is 0.0932, 0.2016, and 1.2238 Debye.

In both the pristine and doped clusters, it is evident that the values for the studied gases and their Eads values (see Tables 1, 2 and 4) follow the same pattern (NH3 > NO2 > NO). When compared to pure AHEX, the values of the dipole moments for TM-AHEX, NO-TM-AHEX, NO2-TM-AHEX, and NH3-TM-AHEX are higher, leading one to the conclusion that the whole dipole moment has been totally exchanged (Tables 1 and 4). The order of their respective magnitudes for NO-TM-AHEX was as follows: NO–Pt-AHEX (2.064) > NO–Pd-AHEX (1.780) > NO–Ni-AHEX (1.77). For NO2-TM-AHEX, it was in the following order: NO2–Pd-AHEX (7.416) > NO2–Pt-AHEX (4.904) > NO–Ni-AHEX (3.974). For NH3-TM-AHEX, it was in the following order: NH3–Ni-AHEX (10.398) > NH3–Pd-AHEX (8.391) > NH3–Pt-AHEX (6.767). The AHEX, TM-AHEX, NO-TM-AHEX, NO2-TM-AHEX, and NH3-TM-AHEX imply that doping has an influence on the dipole moment, which can lead to alterations in the electrical characteristics of the molecular species.

Table S2 lists the results of the Natural Bond Orbital (NBO) study that was done to determine the atomic charges for the gases that were adsorbed as well as the location of the AHEX's adsorbing site. For the molecules of NH3, NO2, and NO, the charges of N, N, and N are −1.167, 0.478, and 0.198 |e|, respectively. The Eads for the adsorbed gases follow a sequence NH3>NO2 > NO for AHEX (see Table 1). The charges of the Ni, Pd, and Pt atoms (see Table 2) for the doped TM- AHEX systems are 0.497, 0.303, and 0.206 |e|, respectively, demonstrating that the higher the positive of charge on the adsorbent core, the larger the adsorption energy. The AHEX's C site has a charge of −0.192 |e| (see Table 2). As a result, the doped AHEX has higher Eads values than the undoped AHEX. Along with the NO-TM-AHEX, NO2-TM-AHEX, and NH3-TM-AHEX complexes, it is intriguing to investigate the electronic configurations of TM atoms in the TM-AHEX complexes. Table S3 provides a list of the electronic configurations for the TM-AHEX, NO-TM-AHEX, NO2-TM-AHEX, and NH3-TM-AHEX complexes. The hybridization between the 4s and 3d orbitals, with a small contribution from the 4p, 5p, and 6p, is what causes the most noticeable change in the electronic configuration in comparison to the free atom configurations. This is clear from the data in Table S3, which was calculated using results from an investigation of the population of natural bond orbitals (NBOs) [88–90]. This investigation shows that the free metal's electronic configuration differs qualitatively from that of the adsorbed metal. The metal's 4s and 3d orbitals exhibit noticeable charge transfer. This is also consistent with the bonding interactions between the transition-metal atom and the regular sites, which are dominated by the polarization of the transition-metal caused by the AHEX's presence.

Fig. 5 and (Fig. S2a-f) show the HOMO and LUMO surfaces, as well as the partial density of states (PDOS) for free NO, NO2, NH3, and the systems NO-AHEX, NO2-AHEX, and NH3-AHEX, providing a more complete analysis of the effect of adsorbed gases on the sorbent clusters.

For the NO gas interaction with AHEX, there is no apparent change in DOS of the AHEX before and after interaction, while there is a little change in the NO gas DOS before and after adsorption, as shown in (Fig. 5a) and (Fig. S2a) whereas the HOMO and LUMO of NO in NO-AHEX shift to −6.85 and −3.95 eV, respectively. In addition, the NO-AHEX system's HOMO surface is −5.45 eV and spreads out throughout the cluster surface, but the LUMO surface is −4.019 eV and is only concentrated in the NO molecule.

For the NO2 gas interaction with AHEX, there is no apparent change in DOS of the AHEX and NO2 gas before and after interaction (Fig. 5b) and (Fig. S2b), Furthermore, the NO2-AHEX system's HOMO surface is −5.50 eV, not localized and dispersed around the cluster surface, whereas the LUMO surface is −3.57 eV, exclusively localized at the NO2 molecule.

As demonstrated in (Fig. 5c) and (Fig. S2c), there is no apparent change in DOS of the AHEX and NH3 gas before and after interaction. The NH3-AHEX system's HOMO -5.35 eV and LUMO -1.70 eV orbitals are localized and are spread only on the cluster surface.

The PDOS for (a) NO–Ni-AHEX, (b) NO2–Ni-AHEX, and (c) NH3–Ni-AHEX are shown in (Fig. S3 a-c). The interaction between the gas and substrate is frequently discussed by analyzing PDOS plots using (Fig. 5, S2, and S3). The PDOS spectra in (Fig. S3 a, b) demonstrates that in the NO–Ni-AHEX and NO2–Ni-AHEX systems, the extent of the occupied states of the NO and NO2 molecules decreased while the intensity of the occupied states of the Ni atom increased. In addition, in the LUMO states, the Ni states of the doped clusters overlap with the gas states (NO and NO2) of the clusters. The geometry of the systems' LUMO surfaces reflects this overlap. As a result, for NO–Ni-AHEX and NO2–Ni-AHEX systems, the HOMOs are moved by −0.175 and −0.299 eV, respectively, while the LUMOs are moved by −1.315 and −1.706 eV, resulting in a −36.55 % and −45.14 % difference in the HOMO-LUMO gap.

As seen in (Fig. S3c) the NH3–Ni-AHEX system exhibits surprising changes in the states of the Ni-AHEX substrate and the NH3 adsorbate. For instance, the highest value at −6.20 eV of the free NH3 gas disappears in the system despite an increase in the intensity of Ni states. Furthermore, the system's HOMO is exclusively ascribed to Ni-AHEX substrate states, but the LUMO is only attributed to AHEX states. This is readily visible on the cluster's HOMO and LUMO surfaces. As a result, the HOMO and LUMO values increased by 0.297 and 0.718 eV, respectively, while the HOMO-LUMO gap expanded by 13.49 %.

The PDOS for NO–Pd-AHEX, NO2–Pd-AHEX, and NH3–Pd-AHEX are shown in Fig. S4. The PDOS spectra in (Fig. S4 a, b) demonstrate that in the NO–Pd-AHEX and NO2–Pd-AHEX systems, the extent of the NO and NO2 molecules' occupied states decreased while the intensity of the Pd atom's occupied states increased. In addition, in the LUMO states, the Pd states of the doped clusters overlap with the gas states (NO and NO2) of the clusters. The geometry of the systems' LUMO surfaces reflects this overlap. As a result, for NO–Pd-AHEX and NO2–Pd-AHEX systems, the HOMOs are moved by −0.070 and −0.314 eV, respectively, while the LUMOs are moved by −1.477 and −3.177 eV, resulting in a −42.76 % and −87.04 % change in the HOMO - LUMO gap.

Differences in the states of the Pd-AHEX substrate and the NH3 adsorbate might explain the persistent interaction between the NH3 and the Pd-AHEX substrate in the NH3–Pd-AHEX system, seen in (Fig. S4c). Conversely, the Pd-AHEX substrate states alone are responsible for the system's HOMO, whilst the AHEX substrate states alone are responsible for the system's LUMO. This is readily visible on the cluster's HOMO and LUMO surfaces. Additionally, there was an increase of 0.56 eV in LUMO and 0.287 eV in HOMO. As a result, the HOMO-LUMO gap widened by 8.48 %.

The PDOS for NO–Pt-AHEX, NO2–Pt-AHEX, and NH3–Pt-AHEX are shown in Fig. S5. The NO–Pt-AHEX and NO2–Pt-AHEX systems' PDOS spectra in (Fig. S5 a, b) demonstrate that although the extent of the Pt atom's occupied states grew, that of the NO and NO2 molecules' occupied states decreased. Furthermore, at the LUMO states, there is an overlap between the gas states (NO and NO2) and the Pt states of the doped clusters. This overlap may be seen in the LUMO surface shapes of the systems. The HOMO-LUMO gap varies by −37.05 % and −35.91 % for NO–Pt-AHEX and NO2–Pt-AHEX systems, respectively, as a result of the HOMOs being moved by −0.113 and −0.166 eV and the LUMOs being moved by −1.248 and 1.265 eV (Fig. S5c). illustrates how the Pt-AHEX substrate and NH3 adsorbate states change significantly in the NH3–Pt-AHEX system. For instance, the system's peak at −6.20 eV for free NH3 gas disappears as the intensity of Pt states rises. Furthermore, the system's HOMO is only returned to the Pt-AHEX substrate's states, while the LUMO is only returned to the AHEX's states. This is readily visible on the cluster's HOMO and LUMO surfaces. As a result, the HOMO and LUMO increased by 0.317 and 0.802 eV, respectively, and the HOMO-LUMO gap widened by 15.83 %.

Furthermore, to investigate the thermodynamic spontaneity of the process, Gibbs free energy changes for the adsorbed gases NO, NO2, and NH3 are computed using the following relation:

The related free energies are G [TM-AHEX + Gases], G [TM-AHEX], and G [Gases]. At T = 298.15 K and P = 1 atm, the thermodynamic parameters Gibbs-free energy change (ΔG), enthalpy change (ΔH), and entropy change (ΔS) for gas molecules adsorbed on TM-doped AHEX GQD systems were computed and displayed in Table 5 . As shown in Table 5, the negative (ΔG) values indicate that gas molecule adsorption on TM -doped AHEX GQD is always thermodynamically advantageous. The higher the value of (ΔG), the stronger the contact between the gas molecule and the substrate. The (ΔH) values are greater than the (ΔG) values, indicating that the enthalpy has been stabilized. The computed Gibbs-free energy change (ΔG) trend for gas molecules adsorbed on TM-doped AHEX GQD.

The nonlinear optical characteristics of polarizability (α) and initial hyperpolarizability (β0) were determined and are shown in Table 6 . The values for AHEX-GQD's polarizability (α) and first hyperpolarizability (β0) derived from Table 6 were approximately 511 and 0.1 au (atomic unit), respectively. The predicted polarizability and first hyperpolarizability trends for NO gas molecules adsorbed on TM-doped AHEX GQD were the same as the adsorption energies (Eads) trends. The following observations are drawn from careful observation:

The determination of Molecular Electrostatic Potential (MEP) contours or surfaces is a crucial step towards the understanding and prediction of molecular activity [91]. It has been a useful tool for studying nucleophilic and electrophilic reactions, especially the “recognition” of one molecule by another [92,93]. Investigations into single-walled nanotube (SWCNT) sensors heavily rely on electrostatic interactions [94,95]. Numerous studies have found that imperfections locations on SWCNTs are chemically more reactive than defect-free locations, despite the use of various theoretical approaches [96–98]. However, AHEX in this situation has received little theoretical attention. MEPs can be positive or high potentials, which are electron-electron-nucleophile reactive, or negative or low potentials, which are electron-electron-nucleophile-reactive. Deep blue is used to indicate the earlier, and deep red is used to indicate the latter. The MEP contours of AHEX, TM-AHEX (TM = Ni, Pd, and Pt), and NO, NO2 and NH3 -TM-AHEX are given in Fig. 6 . As demonstrated, the addition of TM atoms and the adsorption of gas molecules improve the contours' intensity. The MEP contours of the AHEX, TM-AHEX, and gases -TM-AHEX exhibit notable differences in terms of positivity and negativity. On the other hand, the gas molecules and TM atoms are surrounded by the MEP outlines. The MEPs of NH3-TM-AHEX, NO2-TM-AHEX, and NO-TM-AHEX are not the same. However, the results also show that the systems NO-TM-AHEX, NO2-TM-AHEX, and NH3-TM-AHEX, which are indicative of the interactions between gases and doped AHEX-GQD, may be characterized by a molecular electrostatic potential.

The Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra for the isolated AHEX systems and their systems were estimated using time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) with the B3LYP/SDD basis set for excitation from the ground states up to the 15 excited states.

To account for every possible vertical transition in the wavelength range of 300–800 nm, the number of TD-DFT states was selected. The interacting gases' ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra with pristine AHEX and doped Ni-AHEX, Pd-AHEX, and Pt-AHEX systems are displayed in Fig. 7 and Table 7 . The pristine AHEX's maximum absorbance peak (λ max) is found at 357 nm, as shown in (Fig. 7a). This peak correlates to a band-gap transition and corresponds to an excitation energy (E) of 3.48 eV and an oscillator strength (f) of 1.053 Table 7. Additionally, a shift to 743, 565, and 356 nm, respectively, with a drop in intensity as compared to AHEX, is caused by adsorbed gases in the systems NO-AHEX, NO2-AHEX, and NH3-AHEX. It is worth noting that AHEX and NH3-AHEX are ultraviolet-active compounds, whereas NO-AHEX and NO2-AHEX are visible-active compounds. As shown in (Fig. 7b), the (λ max) for NO–Ni-AHEX, NO2–Ni-AHEX, and NH3–Ni-AHEX are found at 515, 510, and 483 nm, respectively, corresponding to E of 2.40, 2.43, and 2.57 eV and f of 0.0615, 0.0147, and 0.0406. Furthermore, the maximum and minimum intensities for NO–Ni-AHEX and NO2–Ni-AHEX are detected. It is clear that NO–Ni-AHEX, NO2–Ni-AHEX, and NH3–Ni-AHEX are active molecules. As shown in (Fig. 7c), the maxima for NO–Pd-AHEX, NO2–Pd-AHEX, and NH3–Pd-AHEX are located at 421, 601, and 427 nm, respectively, corresponding to E of 2.94, 2.07, and 2.90 eV and f of 0.1014, 0.2886, and 0.0068, respectively. Furthermore, the maximum and minimum intensities for NO2–Pd-AHEX and NH3–Pd-AHEX are detected. It is clear that NO–Pd-AHEX, NO2–Pd-AHEX, and NH3–Pd-AHEX are active compounds. As shown in (Fig. 7 d), the (λ max) for NO–Pt-AHEX, NO2–Pt-AHEX, and NH3–Pt-AHEX was found at 604, 447, and 439 nm, respectively, corresponding to E of 2.05, 2.77, and 2.83 eV and f of 0.1008, 0.0275, and 0.0178. Furthermore, the maximum and minimum intensities for NO–Pt-AHEX and NO2–Pt-AHEX are detected. It is clear that NO–Pt-AHEX, NO2–Pt-AHEX, and NH3–Pt-AHEX are active compounds. In summary, the findings show that the doping of Ni, Pd, and Pt converts the UV-active material in the AHEX cluster to visible active material. NO, NO2, and NH3 gas adsorption on Ni-AHEX, Pd-AHEX, and Pt-AHEX causes a significant shift to the max in the visible area. These findings should be useful for designing gas sensors for NO, NO2, and NH3 gases using doped AHEX GQD clusters.

In this work, the effects of doping and the adsorption of NO, NO2, and NH3 gases on the electrical and optical characteristics of AHEX graphene quantum dots are examined using DFT at the B3lyp/SDD level of theory. The impacts of TM (Ni, Pd, and Pt) on the geometric structure and electrical characteristics of AHEX-GQD were examined. In order to demonstrate the stability of the doped Ni-AHEX, Pd-AHEX, and Pt-AHEX, the binding energy, ionization potential, electron affinity, chemical hardness, and softness were determined. Moreover, AHEX has a band gap of 3.662 eV, but Ni, Pd, and Pt doping reduces it to 3.119, 3.288, and 3.063 eV, respectively. The addition of Ni, Pd, and Pt-doped AHEX GQD raises the adsorption energies for NO, NO2, and NH3 gases. The charge moves from the doped AHEX GQD to the NO and NO2 gases. The charge in the NH3 instance is transferred from the gas molecule to the GQD-doped AHEX. For every system, the Gibbs-free energy change (ΔG) is negative, suggesting that these systems contain considerable adsorption. The energy gap narrowed after the NO, NO2, and NH3 adsorption on doped AHEX GQD. Adsorption of NO, NO2, and NH3 gases alters the Eg of Ni-AHEX (−36.55 %, −45.14 %, 13.49 %), Pd-AHEX (−42.76 %, −87.04 %, 8.48 %), and Pt-AHEX (−37.05 %, −35.91 %, 15.83 %), respectively. According to the UV–Vis investigation, the adsorption of NO, NO2, and NH3 gases on the doped AHEX GQD clusters converts from UV-active to visible-active material. This results in a significant shift to the maximum in the visible area. Furthermore, our results show that the doped AHEX GQD clusters may be used as gas sensors for NO, NO2, and NH3 gases because the main standards for evaluating gas sensors are changes in the HOMO-LUMO gap value and the UV–Vis spectrum.
